exercises.title: The actor model for agents
exercises.prompt: AutoGen v0.4 redesigned orchestration around the actor model. Describe it (agents as actors, messages as the only IPC, failure isolation per actor) and why decoupling message delivery from handling gives fault isolation and natural concurrency.
exercises.hint: Actors: independent units with private state. Messages: the only way actors communicate (no shared memory). Each actor processes messages independently → a crash in one doesn't crash others (isolation), and actors run concurrently (no shared state to lock). Decoupling delivery from handling = async, resilient.
exercises.solution:
**The actor model (AutoGen v0.4, Microsoft Research, Jan 2025):**
- **Agents as actors:** each agent is an **independent actor** with its **own private state** — actors don't share memory. An actor is a self-contained unit that processes messages and maintains its own state.
- **Messages as the only IPC:** actors **communicate *only* by exchanging asynchronous messages** — there's **no shared memory, no direct method calls** between actors. To get another actor to do something, you **send it a message**; it processes the message on its own and may send messages back. Message-passing is the sole inter-process-communication mechanism.
- **Failure isolation per actor:** because actors are **independent with private state communicating only via messages**, a **failure (crash, exception) in one actor is contained to that actor** — it doesn't corrupt or crash the others. Each actor can fail, be restarted, or be supervised independently.
AutoGen v0.4's three API layers: **Core** (the actor runtime), **AgentChat** (higher-level conversational agents), **Extensions** (integrations) — Core provides the actor foundation, AgentChat the ergonomic agent API on top.

**Why decoupling message delivery from handling gives fault isolation and natural concurrency:** the key design is that **sending a message is decoupled from processing it** — you send an async message and don't block waiting; the recipient actor processes it independently on its own schedule. This decoupling yields:
- **Fault isolation:** since actors **don't share state and communicate only via messages**, when one actor **fails**, the failure **can't propagate through shared memory** (there is none) — it's **contained to that actor**. The sender isn't blocked on the failed actor (delivery was decoupled from handling), and a supervisor can **restart the failed actor** without affecting the rest. One agent crashing doesn't take down the system — critical for robustness (the multi-agent reliability problem, Lesson 26). Compare a shared-state system where one component's corruption spreads everywhere.
- **Natural concurrency:** because actors have **private state and communicate via async messages (no shared state to lock)**, they can **run concurrently without locks or coordination** — each processes its message queue independently and in parallel. There's **no shared-memory contention** (the classic source of concurrency bugs), so concurrency is the *default*, not something you bolt on. Async message-passing means actors naturally **overlap their work** (one agent reasons while another executes a tool), giving **efficient parallelism for free** (Lesson 3's parallel benefit, structurally built in).
The actor model is the **'fault-isolated concurrency' abstraction** — it's the right shape when you have **multiple agents that should run concurrently and fail independently** (Phase 11.17's framework-matching). (Note: AutoGen v0.4 is now in **maintenance mode** as **Microsoft Agent Framework**, public preview Oct 2025, becomes the successor — but the actor-model insight carries forward.)

exercises.title: Implement an actor-model message loop
exercises.prompt: Implement a stdlib actor-model agent system: actors with private state and a message inbox, an async dispatcher, and message-only communication. Show how a crash in one actor is isolated. Explain the three AutoGen API layers (Core, AgentChat, Extensions).
exercises.hint: Actor: private state + a handle(message) method + an inbox. Dispatcher: routes messages to actor inboxes, runs each actor's handler (isolating exceptions). Crash isolation: wrap each handler in try/except so one actor's failure doesn't stop the dispatcher. Layers: Core (runtime), AgentChat (conversational agents), Extensions (integrations).

tests.desc: the base actor must be subclassed
send delivers to the actor's inbox
run dispatches queued messages to handle
tests.expr: (lambda processed: (lambda a, d: (d.register(a), d.send('w', 'm'), d.run(steps=1), processed)[3])(type('A', (Actor,), {'handle': lambda self, msg, send: processed.append(msg)})('w'), Dispatcher()))([]) == ['m']

exercises.title: Actors vs state graphs vs roles, fault isolation as the differentiator, and when to choose AutoGen
exercises.prompt: Compare the actor model (AutoGen) to LangGraph's state graph (Lesson 13) and CrewAI's roles (Lesson 15) on what each optimizes. Explain why fault isolation and concurrency are the actor model's differentiators, the trade-offs (no global state view, message-ordering complexity), and when concurrent fault-isolated agents are the right shape.
exercises.hint: Actor model: concurrency + fault isolation (no shared state). State graph: durable, inspectable, global state (LangGraph). Roles: role-division (CrewAI). Actor differentiator: independent concurrent agents that fail independently. Trade-offs: no single global state to inspect, message ordering/coordination is harder. Right when many agents run concurrently and must isolate failures.
exercises.solution:
**Actors vs state graphs vs roles (what each optimizes):**
- **Actor model (AutoGen):** optimizes **concurrency and fault isolation** — many independent agents running in parallel, communicating via async messages, each failing independently. Strength: **scale-out, resilience, natural parallelism**. Weakness: **no single global state** (state is distributed across actors).
- **State graph (LangGraph, Lesson 13):** optimizes **durable, inspectable, controllable execution with global state** — an explicit state machine with checkpointing, a single coherent state object, human-in-the-loop. Strength: **durability, inspectability, complex-but-controlled flow**. Weakness: **less natural concurrency** (it's a sequential-ish graph), and the global state can be a bottleneck.
- **Roles (CrewAI, Lesson 15):** optimizes **role-division** — agents as named specialists (researcher, writer) collaborating. Strength: **maps to human-team mental models**, easy to express role-based workflows. Weakness: structured-data handoffs between roles can be awkward (Phase 11.17's leak).
They pick **different core abstractions** (Phase 11.17): **actors (concurrency/isolation) vs state graph (durable global state) vs roles (division of labor)** — match to your problem's shape.

**Why fault isolation and concurrency are the actor model's differentiators:** what **uniquely** distinguishes the actor model from the others is **the combination of (a) no shared state → fault isolation and (b) async message-passing → natural concurrency**:
- **Fault isolation:** in a **state graph (shared global state)**, a failure mid-update can corrupt the shared state, and the graph is a coordinated whole — one component's failure is more entangled. In the **actor model (private state, message-only)**, a failure is **structurally contained to one actor** (no shared state to corrupt, decoupled delivery) — so it's **far more resilient to individual-agent failures** (Lesson 26's failure amplification — actors *limit* amplification by isolation). This is the actor model's signature property: **independent failure**.
- **Concurrency:** the **shared-nothing + async-message** design means actors **run concurrently without locks** — concurrency is the default. State graphs and role-crews can do concurrency but it's less native (shared state needs coordination). The actor model is **built for scale-out parallelism**.
No other framework gives you *both* fault isolation *and* lock-free concurrency as the *fundamental* property — that's the actor model's differentiator.

**The trade-offs:**
- **No global state view:** state is **distributed across actors** (private state, message-passing) — so there's **no single coherent global state to inspect, checkpoint, or reason about** (unlike LangGraph's single state object). Debugging and understanding the system's overall state is **harder** (you must reconstruct it from many actors' states and message logs). Observability is more complex.
- **Message-ordering and coordination complexity:** async message-passing introduces **ordering and timing concerns** — messages may arrive out of order, race conditions in the *logical* flow (not memory) can occur, and **coordinating a result across many actors** (e.g. 'wait for all workers') requires explicit message protocols. The concurrency you get for free brings **distributed-systems complexity** (the classic actor-model trade: easy concurrency, harder reasoning).
- **Eventual consistency:** with distributed state, you may have **temporary inconsistency** across actors.
So the actor model trades **a single inspectable global state (LangGraph) for concurrency + isolation** — you gain resilience and parallelism, you lose the easy global-state view and gain message-coordination complexity.

**When concurrent, fault-isolated agents are the right shape:** choose the actor model (AutoGen) when:
- **You have many agents that should run concurrently** — parallel workers, a swarm of agents, high-throughput multi-agent processing where parallelism matters.
- **Individual agent failures must be isolated** — you can't let one agent's crash take down the system (resilience-critical, long-running, or untrusted-agent settings). Fault isolation is a requirement.
- **Agents are naturally independent** — they have their own state and interact via messages, not by sharing a single workflow state (vs LangGraph's single-state-machine fit).
- **You're building distributed/scale-out** multi-agent systems where the actor model's concurrency and resilience pay off.
**Don't** choose it when you need a **single inspectable durable state with human-in-the-loop and complex sequential control** (→ LangGraph) or a **simple role-division workflow** (→ CrewAI Flows) or a **single agent** (→ a loop or lightweight SDK). The actor model is the right abstraction for **concurrent, resilient, distributed multi-agent systems** — its fault isolation and natural concurrency are exactly what you want when many agents run in parallel and failures must stay local, and exactly what you don't need (extra complexity) for a single sequential controllable agent. As always (Phase 11.17): **match the abstraction to the problem** — actor model for concurrency+isolation, and accept the distributed-state complexity that comes with it.
